title: Rajesh Kumar Sharma v. Infosys BPO Limited & Anr.
keyArguments: The termination of the petitioner was carried out without following the principles of natural justice as no show cause notice was issued prior to dismissal.
The employer failed to conduct a proper domestic enquiry as mandated under the Standing Orders and the terms of the employment contract.
The alleged misconduct of sharing confidential data was not substantiated by any cogent or admissible evidence placed on record.
The petitioner had an unblemished service record of eleven years and the punishment of dismissal was disproportionate to the alleged misconduct.
The respondent company suppressed material facts before the Labour Court and obtained an ex-parte order which was liable to be set aside.
judgmentSummary: The High Court of Delhi set aside the order of termination passed by Infosys BPO Limited against the petitioner Rajesh Kumar Sharma, finding that the termination was in gross violation of natural justice and the principles of fair procedure. The court directed reinstatement of the petitioner with full back wages for the period of wrongful unemployment, along with continuity of service and all attendant benefits. The court held that an employer cannot dispense with a domestic enquiry merely on grounds of urgency or commercial sensitivity, and that the right to be heard is a fundamental component of employment jurisprudence in India.
judgmentText:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI
W.P.(C) No. 7841/2022

RAJESH KUMAR SHARMA ... PETITIONER
VERSUS
INFOSYS BPO LIMITED & ANR. ... RESPONDENTS

Date of Decision: 14th March, 2023

HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ANIL KUMAR VERMA

JUDGMENT

1. The present writ petition has been filed under Article 226 of the Constitution of India challenging the order of termination dated 03rd September, 2021 passed by Respondent No. 1, Infosys BPO Limited, whereby the services of the Petitioner were terminated with immediate effect on grounds of alleged misconduct involving unauthorized sharing of client data.

2. The Petitioner, who joined the services of Respondent No. 1 in the year 2010 as a Senior Process Associate, contends that the impugned order of termination was passed in flagrant violation of the principles of natural justice, inasmuch as no show cause notice was served upon him prior to dismissal, and no domestic enquiry was conducted in accordance with the Certified Standing Orders applicable to the establishment.

3. Learned counsel for the Petitioner, Mr. Arvind Tiwari, has vehemently argued that the Respondent-employer has not placed on record any documentary or electronic evidence to substantiate the charge of data leakage. He further submits that the petitioner was not afforded any reasonable opportunity to examine the witnesses or to cross-examine the evidence relied upon by the management.

4. Per contra, learned counsel appearing for the Respondent-company, Ms. Priya Sehgal, has argued that the nature of the misconduct was grave and involved breach of confidentiality agreements, and that the internal investigation conducted by the company's security team was sufficient compliance with procedural requirements.

5. Having heard learned counsel for both parties and having perused the record carefully, this Court is of the considered opinion that the contentions advanced by the Respondent are wholly untenable in law.

6. It is well-settled by a catena of decisions of the Hon'ble Supreme Court of India, including the landmark judgment in Central Inland Water Transport Corporation Ltd. v. Brojo Nath Ganguly, (1986) 3 SCC 156, that any order of termination passed in violation of natural justice is void ab initio and cannot be sustained. The requirement of issuing a charge sheet, conducting a domestic enquiry, and providing an opportunity of being heard to the delinquent employee is not a mere formality but a substantive right which cannot be abridged by any internal policy of the employer.

7. In the instant case, the record clearly reveals that no charge sheet was ever issued to the Petitioner. The so-called internal investigation report was prepared unilaterally by the management without any participation of the Petitioner. The Petitioner was called to the human resources office on 03rd September, 2021, and was handed a termination letter without any prior intimation. Such conduct of the employer is a textbook case of violation of the audi alteram partem rule.

8. Furthermore, this Court notes that the Petitioner had served the Respondent company for over eleven years without a single adverse entry in his service record. The punishment of outright dismissal, even if the charges were to be assumed to be true, which they are not, would be grossly disproportionate and would fall foul of the doctrine of proportionality as enunciated by the Supreme Court in B.C. Chaturvedi v. Union of India, (1995) 6 SCC 749.

9. In light of the foregoing discussion, this Court allows the present writ petition. The order of termination dated 03rd September, 2021 is hereby quashed and set aside. The Respondent-company is directed to reinstate the Petitioner in service forthwith in his original post or an equivalent post, with full back wages, continuity of service, and all consequential service benefits, within a period of four weeks from the date of this judgment.

10. The Respondents shall also pay costs of Rs. 50,000/- to the Petitioner.

Order accordingly.

(ANIL KUMAR VERMA)
JUDGE
MARCH 14, 2023
